patterns.what_to_look_for: Admin/management routes mounted under predictable prefixes (/admin, /administrator, /manage, /console, /wp-admin, Django /admin/, mount Sidekiq::Web, Bull-Board) whose only protection is not being linked in the UI: no auth middleware/guard wraps the route group, or auth is applied per-action and missed on some endpoints.
source_indicators: app.use('/admin', adminRouter) / mount Sidekiq::Web with no surrounding auth middleware
Admin controller lacking class-level [Authorize(Roles="Admin")] / @login_required while siblings have it
Route marked 'internal only' in a comment with no enforcement
Mounted dashboard (Bull-Board, Flower, Django admin) registered before/outside the auth chain
expose_admin / debug admin flag wired true in non-dev config
blackbox_indicators: GET /admin, /administrator, /manage, /wp-admin returns 200 admin UI instead of 302/401/403
Framework default admin (Django /admin/, /sidekiq, Bull-Board) reachable with anonymous session
Route brute-force reveals admin paths serving content to an unauthenticated client
Admin path returns 200 while the app UI never links it
evidence_required: Exact admin URL + raw request issued with no/low-priv session
Response showing privileged content or an available privileged control
Demonstration that no auth redirect/401/403 occurs
White-box: route registration line missing an auth guard
false_positive_checks: Confirm the 200 response isn't a login page served inline
Check for upstream proxy auth (mTLS, basic auth, IP allowlist) not visible in app code
Confirm a privileged action actually executes, not just a shell that 403s on submit
Rule out a honeypot/decoy admin path
patterns.suggested_validation_task: Send an unauthenticated GET to the discovered admin path and record the status code plus whether a privileged control renders.
patterns.reporting_guidance: Report as broken access control on the admin route; include the exact path, the anonymous request, and the privileged element exposed.

patterns.what_to_look_for: Debug/profiler/diagnostic surfaces left enabled in production: Spring Boot Actuator (/actuator/env, /heapdump, /threaddump, /mappings), Django DEBUG=True + Werkzeug console, Symfony /_profiler & /_fragment, Laravel Telescope/Ignition, Rails /rails/info, phpinfo(), Go /debug/pprof, or GraphQL introspection/playground in prod.
source_indicators: DEBUG = True / APP_DEBUG=true / app.debug=True in committed or prod config
management.endpoints.web.exposure.include=* without spring-security on actuator
Telescope/Debugbar/Ignition listed under require (not require-dev) in composer.json
import _ "net/http/pprof" / app.use('/debug', ...) / registered /__debug__
GraphQL introspection:true or playground enabled unconditionally
blackbox_indicators: /actuator, /actuator/env, /actuator/heapdump returns JSON config or a binary dump
Verbose stack-trace page with an interactive console (Werkzeug PIN, Symfony profiler)
/_profiler, /telescope, /rails/info/routes, or phpinfo() output reachable anonymously
GraphQL __schema introspection succeeds or /graphiql / playground loads in prod
evidence_required: URL + response proving the debug surface (env vars, stack trace, heap dump, schema)
Sensitive data or actionable capability shown (secrets, tokens, code-exec console)
Note whether the surface requires any authentication
false_positive_checks: Confirm the endpoint is on the public host, not an internal mgmt port behind a firewall
Check actuator value sanitization (env masked as ******) before rating severity
Verify the host is production, not a staging/dev environment
Confirm GraphQL introspection isn't intentionally public for a documented public API
patterns.suggested_validation_task: Request the debug/actuator/profiler URL anonymously and capture whether sensitive internals or an interactive console render.
patterns.reporting_guidance: Report as debug-endpoint exposure; quantify the leaked data and flag any code-exec console (Werkzeug/Spring) as critical.

patterns.what_to_look_for: Privileged operations gated only by the frontend hiding the control: the backend REST endpoint or GraphQL mutation performs the admin action without re-checking role/permission server-side. Classic vertical privilege escalation via forced browsing to the admin API.
source_indicators: Admin API handler with no requireRole('admin')/policy call, relying on a separate admin SPA
Authorization done only in the view ({isAdmin && <Button/>}) with no matching server guard
Permission check present on the GET (render) but absent on the mutating POST/PUT/DELETE
Shared service method invoked by both admin and user controllers without its own authz
Endpoint like POST /api/admin/users/{id}/role lacking a role assertion
blackbox_indicators: Low-priv session calling an admin-only endpoint returns 200 and performs the action
Admin endpoints present in the JS bundle / OpenAPI / GraphQL schema but absent from low-priv UI
Same payload succeeds with a normal user's token as with an admin's token
403 on UI navigation but 200 on the direct API call
evidence_required: Two-account proof: a low-priv token successfully invokes the admin action
Request/response showing the state change or privileged data returned
White-box: source location of the unguarded handler
false_positive_checks: Confirm the action truly executed (re-read state) vs a 200 with silent server-side deny
Verify the 'low-priv' account wasn't accidentally granted the role
Check for an object/row-level filter that limits real impact
Rule out an idempotent no-op endpoint
patterns.suggested_validation_task: Replay the admin endpoint with a freshly created low-privilege account's token and verify the privileged effect actually occurred.
patterns.reporting_guidance: Report as vertical privilege escalation; lead with the two-account proof and the missing server-side role check.

patterns.what_to_look_for: Privilege or admin status decided from a client-controllable signal: a request header (X-Admin, X-Internal, trusted X-Forwarded-For), a body/query param (isAdmin, role, admin=1), an editable cookie, an unverified/mass-assignable JWT or profile claim, or an internal-only route gated solely by a spoofable header.
source_indicators: if request.headers['X-Admin'] / X-Internal-Request used for authorization
role/permission read from req.body/query/cookie instead of the server session
Mass assignment of is_admin/role/is_staff from a user-update payload with no allowlist
JWT custom claim role/admin trusted without verifying it was server-issued (or alg=none accepted)
Internal route gated by 'if ip in INTERNAL_CIDR' while behind a proxy that forwards client headers
blackbox_indicators: Adding X-Admin:true or X-Forwarded-For:127.0.0.1 unlocks an admin response
Sending isAdmin=true / role=admin in update or registration grants elevated access
Tampering a base64/JWT claim and replaying still authorizes the request
Internal/admin route returns 200 only when a spoofed internal header is supplied
evidence_required: Baseline (denied) vs modified (granted) request diff isolating the single client-controlled field
Proof the elevated state persists or the privileged action runs
White-box: source line trusting the client value
false_positive_checks: Confirm the header isn't stripped/overwritten by the real edge proxy (test through prod edge, not origin)
Verify the JWT signature is genuinely unverified vs server re-signs/validates
Check the mass-assigned field is actually honored by authz, not merely stored
Ensure the change isn't reverted by a later authoritative server-side read
patterns.suggested_validation_task: Resend the request with the suspected client-controlled privilege field/header toggled and compare the authorization outcome.
patterns.reporting_guidance: Report as authorization based on untrusted client input; name the exact field/header and show the before/after access diff.

patterns.what_to_look_for: Built-in superuser bypasses: still-active default credentials (admin/admin), hardcoded backdoor accounts or tokens, a magic header/param that skips auth, a dev impersonation / 'login as user' feature reachable in prod, or a seeded admin with a known password.
source_indicators: Hardcoded credentials/tokens in the auth path (if user=='support' && pass=='...')
if token == 'BACKDOOR' / MASTER_KEY env compared to grant access
Seed or migration creating an admin with a static password
Impersonation endpoint (/impersonate, login_as, become) without a strict guard or audit
Feature flag ALLOW_SUPERUSER / GOD_MODE evaluated outside a dev-only guard
blackbox_indicators: Vendor default creds (admin/admin, root/root) successfully log in
A magic value/header grants access matching a known CVE backdoor for the product/version
Impersonation / login-as URL works for a non-admin or without re-authentication
A documented default API key/token is still valid
evidence_required: Successful authentication/session via the default or backdoor path
Identification of the static credential/token/flag (source or known CVE)
Proof of a privileged capability exercised with that access
false_positive_checks: Confirm default creds weren't rotated (attempt login only, stop before any destructive action)
Verify the backdoor branch is reachable in the prod build, not dev-only / compiled out
Check the impersonation feature actually requires an existing admin (then it's intended)
Rule out a honeytoken/canary account
patterns.suggested_validation_task: Attempt a single login with the documented default credential and stop immediately at the authenticated landing page.
patterns.reporting_guidance: Report as backdoor/default-credential access; cite the static secret location or CVE and the privileged session obtained.

patterns.what_to_look_for: A privileged operation enforced on the primary path but exposed unguarded on an alternate channel: a management/actuator port on a separate listener, internal RPC/gRPC/queue worker callable directly, a legacy /v1 or mobile API missing the web's admin middleware, or JMX/metrics consoles reachable publicly.
source_indicators: Admin authz in web middleware but absent on the API/GraphQL/gRPC handler of the same action
Separate management server (management.server.port, JMX, debug listener) without auth
Legacy /v1 or mobile controller duplicating admin logic without the newer guard
Background/queue consumer or internal RPC performing privileged ops on implicit trust
Admin route registered before/outside the auth middleware chain
blackbox_indicators: Admin action blocked on the web UI but succeeds via /api, /graphql, or /v1 equivalent
Management/metrics/JMX port (e.g. 8081, 9990, actuator on an alt port) reachable from internet
Mobile/legacy endpoint performs the privileged op without the newer authorization
Different status (403 vs 200) for the same operation across channels
evidence_required: Request proving the privileged op succeeds on the alternate channel while denied on the primary
Mapping of the two channels to the same underlying privileged action
White-box: config/source showing the guard exists on one path only
false_positive_checks: Confirm both channels truly hit the same privileged operation, not a read-only twin
Verify the alternate port isn't firewalled at the network edge
Check the alternate channel isn't enforcing authz at a different layer (gateway/mTLS)
Ensure the legacy endpoint isn't deprecated/disabled in production
patterns.suggested_validation_task: Invoke the privileged action through the alternate channel (API/GraphQL/legacy/mgmt port) with the same low-priv identity and compare to the primary path's denial.
patterns.reporting_guidance: Report as inconsistent authorization across channels for a privileged operation; show the allow-vs-deny diff and name both paths.
